prompt: Develop an argument explaining how Atlantic commerce caused a consumer revolution in British North America from 1650 to 1754.
student_response: Atlantic commerce was a big part of life in British North America. People living in the colonies wanted things, and merchants wanted to make money by selling those things. Ships crossed the ocean and brought items that colonists could buy. This meant that there were more goods around than there had been before. Some people could afford more than other people, so buying things also showed who had money. Commerce involved sellers, buyers, sailors, and people who worked to produce goods. The colonies also sent materials away and received finished products back. Over time this made shopping more common in colonial life. It also connected people who lived far apart because an item could travel through several places before reaching a house. There were stores and advertisements that encouraged people to be interested in new products. Families might replace older belongings or try to have the same styles as other families. The consumer revolution happened during this time and Atlantic commerce was related to it. Trade kept growing, and the lives of colonists were affected by the things moving across the Atlantic.

feedback.thesis: The response only says Atlantic commerce was related to the consumer revolution; it does not make a specific causal claim or establish a line of reasoning.
feedback.contextualization: The discussion of colonies sending materials and receiving products remains part of the prompt's commerce topic rather than developing broader historical context.
feedback.evidence: Stores, advertisements, goods, and shopping are mentioned only generally, without at least two specific historical examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response does use causation by linking Atlantic shipments to greater availability of goods and more common shopping.

thesis: The consumer revolution happened during this time and Atlantic commerce was related to it.
contextualization: The colonies also sent materials away and received finished products back.
evidence: There were stores and advertisements that encouraged people to be interested in new products.
analysis_reasoning: Ships crossed the ocean and brought items that colonists could buy. This meant that there were more goods around than there had been before.

feedback.thesis: The essay makes a defensible causal claim that Atlantic shipping increased available goods and encouraged more buying, establishing a basic line of reasoning.
feedback.contextualization: The opening stays within the prompt's commerce-and-consumption topic and does not describe a broader historical setting.
feedback.evidence: Stores, advertisements, and exchanged goods are relevant, but they remain generalized categories rather than at least two specific historical examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses causation by linking Atlantic movement of goods to availability, shopping, status display, and shared styles, but it does not develop a complex argument.

thesis: Ships crossed the ocean and brought items that colonists could buy. This meant that there were more goods around than there had been before.
contextualization: Atlantic commerce was a big part of life in British North America.
evidence: The colonies also sent materials away and received finished products back.
There were stores and advertisements that encouraged people to be interested in new products.
analysis_reasoning: Over time this made shopping more common in colonial life.
Some people could afford more than other people, so buying things also showed who had money.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in political participation in British mainland colonies from 1619 to 1754.
student_response: Political participation in the British mainland colonies changed from being mostly a small local activity controlled by landowning men into a more regular part of public life for a wider group of white men, although it remained continuous in excluding women, enslaved people, Native people, and poor men and in allowing wealthy elites to have the most influence. At the beginning, government was close to the community. Men met to talk about taxes, roads, land, and other local needs. As settlements grew, more offices and elected groups developed. This gave colonists experience choosing representatives and expecting those representatives to listen to complaints. Participation was not democratic in the modern meaning. Voting rules were based on property and gender, and important families usually had better education and more connections. The average person might participate sometimes but still defer to a richer neighbor. This continuty mattered because the social order stayed unequal even when political habits expanded. Over the years population grew and settlements spread. More white men owned some land and believed that ownership gave them a stake in decisions. Elections could become competative, with candidates seeking support from people in their district. Printed arguments and public discussion also made politics more visible. These developments changed participation because government was no longer only something distant officials did. At the same time, most people in the colonies still had no legal political voice. Women could discuss public issues inside households but did not vote. Laborers without enough property could be affected by laws but not choose lawmakers. Enslaved people were governed through force instead of consent. Political participation thus expanded inside a narrow category instead of becoming equal. Later American politics shows how incomplete this colonial change was. The Constitution eventually created a national voting system in which every adult citizen directly voted for the president, and the nineteenth amendment gave voting rights soon after independence. These later changes show that colonial people were starting a long democratic process, even though those examples occured outside the colonial period. Overall, the major change was greater involvement by ordinary property-owning white men through elections and local government, while the major continuity was that wealth, race, and gender decided who counted as part of the political community. Colonists talked more about representation, but representation did not mean everyone. Even among eligible men, participation varied because travel, work, and local customs affected whether someone attended a meeting or voted. Expansion was real but uneven.

feedback.thesis: The opening makes a defensible continuity-and-change claim: participation expanded among white men while exclusion and elite influence persisted.
feedback.contextualization: The essay frames the argument through early local government and the later growth of population and settlements.
feedback.evidence: Property-based voting, local meetings, elections, printed debate, and the exclusion of women, laborers, and enslaved people are used to support both change and continuity.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Continuity and change structure the full argument, which also qualifies expansion by discussing legal exclusion, elite influence, and uneven participation.

thesis: Political participation in the British mainland colonies changed from being mostly a small local activity controlled by landowning men into a more regular part of public life for a wider group of white men, although it remained continuous in excluding women, enslaved people, Native people, and poor men and in allowing wealthy elites to have the most influence.
contextualization: At the beginning, government was close to the community. Men met to talk about taxes, roads, land, and other local needs.
evidence: Voting rules were based on property and gender, and important families usually had better education and more connections.
Elections could become competative, with candidates seeking support from people in their district. Printed arguments and public discussion also made politics more visible.
analysis_reasoning: Political participation thus expanded inside a narrow category instead of becoming equal.
Expansion was real but uneven.

feedback.thesis: The opening clearly argues that participation expanded among white property-owning men while exclusions and elite influence continued.
feedback.contextualization: The essay begins with colonial political participation itself rather than framing it with broader developments outside the prompt's immediate subject.
feedback.evidence: Property and gender voting restrictions, local meetings, elections, printed debate, and officeholding are used to support both expansion and continuity.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Continuity and change structure the entire response, which also qualifies expansion by race, gender, wealth, region, and practical access to participation.

thesis: Political participation in the British mainland colonies changed from being mostly a small local activity controlled by landowning men into a more regular part of public life for a wider group of white men, although it remained continuous in excluding women, enslaved people, Native people, and poor men and in allowing wealthy elites to have the most influence.
contextualization: At the beginning, government was close to the community.
evidence: Voting rules were based on property and gender, and important families usually had better education and more connections.
Elections could become competative, with candidates seeking support from people in their district. Printed arguments and public discussion also made politics more visible.
analysis_reasoning: Overall, the major change was greater involvement by ordinary property-owning white men through elections and local government, while the major continuity was that wealth, race, and gender decided who counted as part of the political community.

prompt: Develop an argument evaluating continuity and change in Indigenous diplomatic strategies toward European colonies from 1607 to 1754.
student_response: Before permanent English settlements, Europeans had already crossed the Atlantic and created competing empires. The Columbian Exchange brought diseases, animals, and trade goods, while Native nations still controlled most of the land. Europeans needed Native knowledge and allies because the newcomers were few and did not understand the continent. This set up diplomacy once colonies appeared. There were meetings and agreements. Trading. Gift giving. Sometimes war. Native peoples tried different things depending on what was happening near them. They could work with one European group and then work with another one later. They did not all act as one group. The Iroquois Confederacy used the Covenant Chain with the English to preserve an alliance and gain influence in trade and warfare. A specific diplomatic relationship. Other nations tried to stay neutral when Europeans fought. Or moved their villages. Colonists kept wanting land, which made agreements hard to keep. There was continuity because diplomacy continued, but there was also change, and there were many strategies. It is hard to decide which one was most important. Native leaders spoke at councils and expected gifts. Europeans wrote treaties. Different understandings of land. Arguments afterward. Much later the Cherokee took a court case all the way to the Supreme Court and President Jackson ignored it, leading to the Trail of Tears. That shows another diplomatic and legal strategy, although it happened well after 1754. It was not always successful. During the colonial years trade could make an alliance useful, while settlement could make the same Europeans threatening. Some Native nations fought the colonists and some helped them. Sometimes both at different times. European rivalry gave Native diplomats choices, but increasing colonial population reduced those choices. Overall there were diplomatic strategies toward colonies from 1607 to 1754. Alliances, neutrality, trade, and resistance. Changes happened as Europeans became stronger. Continuities happened too.

feedback.thesis: The conclusion makes a minimally defensible claim that diplomacy continued while strategies changed as European power grew.
feedback.contextualization: The essay explains European imperial competition, the Columbian Exchange, Native control of land, and European dependence on Native knowledge before sustained colonial diplomacy.
feedback.evidence: The Covenant Chain and council gift giving provide relevant examples, but only the Covenant Chain is specifically developed in support of the argument.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses continuity and change by contrasting persistent diplomacy with changing choices as colonial population and power increased, but it does not develop a complex argument.

thesis: Changes happened as Europeans became stronger. Continuities happened too.
contextualization: Before permanent English settlements, Europeans had already crossed the Atlantic and created competing empires. The Columbian Exchange brought diseases, animals, and trade goods, while Native nations still controlled most of the land.
evidence: The Iroquois Confederacy used the Covenant Chain with the English to preserve an alliance and gain influence in trade and warfare.
Native leaders spoke at councils and expected gifts.
analysis_reasoning: European rivalry gave Native diplomats choices, but increasing colonial population reduced those choices.

feedback.thesis: The essay ultimately argues that European rivalry initially created diplomatic options while rising colonial population reduced them, identifying both continuity and change.
feedback.contextualization: It accurately situates diplomacy in Atlantic imperial competition, the Columbian Exchange, and initial European dependence on Native knowledge and allies.
feedback.evidence: The Covenant Chain is one specific relevant example, but the remaining references are generic or outside the period, so the required two specific examples are not present.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses continuity-and-change reasoning through changing alliances, neutrality, trade, and resistance, but the fragmented development does not demonstrate complexity.

thesis: European rivalry gave Native diplomats choices, but increasing colonial population reduced those choices.
contextualization: Before permanent English settlements, Europeans had already crossed the Atlantic and created competing empires. The Columbian Exchange brought diseases, animals, and trade goods, while Native nations still controlled most of the land. Europeans needed Native knowledge and allies because the newcomers were few and did not understand the continent.
evidence: The Iroquois Confederacy used the Covenant Chain with the English to preserve an alliance and gain influence in trade and warfare.
analysis_reasoning: During the colonial years trade could make an alliance useful, while settlement could make the same Europeans threatening.
Changes happened as Europeans became stronger. Continuities happened too.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the most important causes of the emergence of the first party system from 1787 to 1800.
student_response: The first party system emerged mainly because national leaders disagreed about how powerful the new federal government should be and whose economic interests it should serve, while arguments over foreign affairs turned those disagreements into rival political organizations. Supporters of a more energetic national government believed that a young country needed order. They generally trusted educated and wealthy citizens to guide public decisions. They wanted the government to support business, public credit, and connections among different regions. People on the other side worried that these goals would create an elite class that could control ordinary farmers. They favored a narrower reading of national power and talked more about local independence. The economic disagreement became political because every major proposal seemed to raise the same question: should the central government act broadly, or should it leave more choices to states and individuals? Neither side thought of itself as creating a dangerous faction. Each claimed it was protecting the principles of the Revolution and accused the other group of betraying them. This made compromise harder. Foreign events added another division. One group valued stability and trade with a powerful European nation, while the other sympathized with a foreign revolution that appeared to continue the struggle against monarchy. Newspapers repeated charges and made the disagreement personal. Voters began to recognize two competing sets of ideas even when formal party organization was still developing. These divisions were not caused simply by leaders disliking one another. The basic cause was an unresolved problem left by the creation of the new government: national power had been strengthened, but Americans had not agreed on what that strength should be used for. Economic interests deepened the argument, and international conflict made neutrality seem partisan. As a result, loose groups became parties because people needed lasting organizations to defend opposing visions of the republic.

feedback.thesis: The thesis makes a defensible causal claim that disagreements over federal power and economic interests drove party formation, with foreign affairs intensifying the division.
feedback.contextualization: The essay moves directly into the causes of party division and does not develop broader historical circumstances beyond the prompt's political problem.
feedback.evidence: It describes policy, economic, foreign, and newspaper disputes only in generalized terms and supplies no specific historical examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: Causation structures the essay, which explains how unresolved constitutional disagreement, economic interests, and foreign conflict turned loose groups into parties.

thesis: The first party system emerged mainly because national leaders disagreed about how powerful the new federal government should be and whose economic interests it should serve, while arguments over foreign affairs turned those disagreements into rival political organizations.
contextualization: The basic cause was an unresolved problem left by the creation of the new government: national power had been strengthened, but Americans had not agreed on what that strength should be used for.
evidence: One group valued stability and trade with a powerful European nation, while the other sympathized with a foreign revolution that appeared to continue the struggle against monarchy.
analysis_reasoning: Economic interests deepened the argument, and international conflict made neutrality seem partisan. As a result, loose groups became parties because people needed lasting organizations to defend opposing visions of the republic.

feedback.thesis: The thesis prioritizes disagreement over federal power and economic interests while explaining foreign affairs as a force that converted disagreements into party organizations.
feedback.contextualization: The response begins with the causes named in its argument and does not describe a broader setting beyond the immediate party conflict.
feedback.evidence: Public credit and business policy, competing constitutional visions, reactions to European revolution and trade, newspapers, and voter organization support the causal argument.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The essay ranks and connects ideological, economic, and foreign causes while presenting both sides as rival interpretations of Revolutionary principles.

contextualization: Supporters of a more energetic national government believed that a young country needed order.

The basic cause was an unresolved problem left by the creation of the new government: national power had been strengthened, but Americans had not agreed on what that strength should be used for. Economic interests deepened the argument, and international conflict made neutrality seem partisan.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing Hamiltonian and Jeffersonian visions for the United States from 1789 to 1800.
student_response: The American Revolution left the United States independent but also left debt and suspicion of strong government. Under the Articles of Confederation the national government had trouble raising money and making states cooperate. The Constitution was created in this situation and produced arguments about what republican government should look like. Hamilton and Jefferson were important people who had different visions. Hamilton thought the country needed strength. He was connected to merchants and cities and believed credit was important. The National Bank was part of his program because it could hold government funds and support the financial system. Jefferson did not like this plan. He was more favorable to farmers and thought concentrated power could become dangerous. These are two sides of the issue. There were also similarities because both had served the new nation and both wanted it to succeed. One looked toward manufacturing and the other looked toward farming. It is often said that Hamilton liked rich people and Jefferson liked common people. This was probably true because bankers always support Hamilton's type of government, while all farmers naturally support Jefferson's type. Their ideas went into every part of politics. Hamilton believed the Constitution allowed useful powers even if every power was not listed. Jefferson read the document more narrowly. This was a disagreement. Foreign affairs were happening too. The French Revolution caused excitement and fear in America. Jefferson liked France because France had helped the United States, and Hamilton liked Britain because trade was needed. There were debates about neutrality. Some Americans wore symbols and wrote newspaper attacks. Hamilton's system was modern and Jefferson's was old fashioned, but Jefferson was also more democratic. Hamilton wanted the president to be like a king, while Jefferson did not want any national government at all. They argued in the cabinet and eventually political groups appeared. One group tended to live in commercial places and another in agricultural places. The visions can be compared by economics, government, and foreign relations. Hamilton supported one approach and Jefferson supported another approach. In the end the election of 1828 proved Jefferson's vision because Andrew Jackson created democracy for ordinary men, though that took place much later. Political parties still exist now, which shows these disagreements were important. There were many differences between Hamilton and Jefferson from 1789 to 1800, as well as the similarity that they both had visions for the United States.

feedback.thesis: Across its comparison, the essay claims that Hamilton favored national strength, finance, manufacturing, and broad constitutional power while Jefferson favored farmers and narrower power; it also notes their shared desire for national success.
feedback.contextualization: The opening explains Revolutionary debt, suspicion of strong government, weaknesses under the Articles, and the Constitution's creation.
feedback.evidence: The National Bank, constitutional interpretation, the French Revolution, neutrality, and differing orientations toward Britain and France are used to compare the visions, despite some exaggerated claims elsewhere.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response compares the two visions across economics, government, foreign affairs, and social bases and includes a similarity, meeting the multiple-theme standard.

thesis: Hamilton believed the Constitution allowed useful powers even if every power was not listed. Jefferson read the document more narrowly.
There were also similarities because both had served the new nation and both wanted it to succeed.
contextualization: Under the Articles of Confederation the national government had trouble raising money and making states cooperate. The Constitution was created in this situation and produced arguments about what republican government should look like.
evidence: The National Bank was part of his program because it could hold government funds and support the financial system. Jefferson did not like this plan.
Jefferson liked France because France had helped the United States, and Hamilton liked Britain because trade was needed.
analysis_reasoning: The visions can be compared by economics, government, and foreign relations.

feedback.thesis: Across several sentences, the essay contrasts Hamilton's commercial, credit-centered, stronger-government vision with Jefferson's agrarian and narrower-government vision.
feedback.contextualization: It accurately frames their dispute through Revolutionary debt, suspicion of power, Articles weaknesses, and the Constitution's creation.
feedback.evidence: The National Bank, constitutional interpretation, the French Revolution, neutrality, British trade, and regional political support are used in the comparison despite some overstatements.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response uses comparison across economics, government, and foreign relations, but inaccurate absolutes and list-like treatment prevent a complex understanding.

thesis: Hamilton thought the country needed strength. He was connected to merchants and cities and believed credit was important.
Jefferson did not like this plan. He was more favorable to farmers and thought concentrated power could become dangerous.
contextualization: The American Revolution left the United States independent but also left debt and suspicion of strong government. Under the Articles of Confederation the national government had trouble raising money and making states cooperate. The Constitution was created in this situation and produced arguments about what republican government should look like.
evidence: The National Bank was part of his program because it could hold government funds and support the financial system.
Hamilton believed the Constitution allowed useful powers even if every power was not listed. Jefferson read the document more narrowly.
The French Revolution caused excitement and fear in America. Jefferson liked France because France had helped the United States, and Hamilton liked Britain because trade was needed.
analysis_reasoning: The visions can be compared by economics, government, and foreign relations.
Hamilton wanted the president to be like a king, while Jefferson did not want any national government at all.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the most important causes of the market revolution from 1800 to 1848.
student_response: The most important causes of the market revolution were improvements in transportation and labor-saving technology, because they made it cheaper to move and produce goods, while population growth and a stronger desire for profit provided secondary support. Transportation changed how far a seller could reach. When roads were slow, most people traded close to home and made many items for themselves. Better routes connected farms to towns and towns to ports. A crop that once would spoil or cost too much to ship could become something grown for sale. People therefore paid more attention to prices in other places. Technology had a similar effect inside production. Machines allowed one worker to make more in less time, and businesses gathered workers together instead of relying only on separate households. Production became faster and more regular. This encouraged owners to invest more money and encouraged customers to expect goods they had not made themselves. The transportation and technology changes happened gradually in the early part of the century, although some regions changed before others and exact timing is hard to determine. Population growth mattered because more people meant more workers and buyers. Towns became larger, and migration placed farms on new land. Business owners also became more willing to borrow, invest, and compete. These causes alone would not have transformed the economy without the physical ability to produce and transport goods, however. A large population with poor connections still creates mostly local markets. A new business attitude without useful machines still limits output. Transportation and technology were therefore the leading causes because they turned other changes into an interconnected market. The results could be seen in more wage work, more commercial farming, and more dependence on cash. Families purchased things that they previously produced and workers followed jobs away from home. Not everyone benefited equally, since owners could gain wealth while workers faced long days. Still, the central cause was the practical ability to make goods quickly and send them to distant consumers, which allowed scattered local exchanges to become a national market.

feedback.thesis: The thesis ranks transportation and labor-saving technology above population growth and profit seeking and gives a defensible causal rationale.
feedback.contextualization: The essay describes the earlier condition of slow routes, local exchange, and household production to frame the later market transformation.
feedback.evidence: Routes, machines, population, towns, and investment remain generalized categories; the response gives no specific historical developments or examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The causal argument is sophisticated: it ranks factors, explains how they interacted, uses counterfactual reasoning, and acknowledges uneven regional timing and unequal effects.

thesis: The most important causes of the market revolution were improvements in transportation and labor-saving technology, because they made it cheaper to move and produce goods, while population growth and a stronger desire for profit provided secondary support.
contextualization: When roads were slow, most people traded close to home and made many items for themselves.
evidence: Better routes connected farms to towns and towns to ports.
Machines allowed one worker to make more in less time
analysis_reasoning: These causes alone would not have transformed the economy without the physical ability to produce and transport goods, however. A large population with poor connections still creates mostly local markets. A new business attitude without useful machines still limits output.

feedback.thesis: The thesis prioritizes transportation and labor-saving technology and explains why population and profit desire were secondary.
feedback.contextualization: The essay begins directly with its causal claim and does not establish a broader historical context for the market revolution.
feedback.evidence: Routes, machines, population, towns, and investment are discussed only generically; the essay does not provide two specific historical examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response offers sophisticated causal ranking, counterfactual tests, regional qualification, and links among production, transportation, population, and investment.

thesis: The most important causes of the market revolution were improvements in transportation and labor-saving technology, because they made it cheaper to move and produce goods, while population growth and a stronger desire for profit provided secondary support.
contextualization: Transportation changed how far a seller could reach.
evidence: Better routes connected farms to towns and towns to ports.
Machines allowed one worker to make more in less time, and businesses gathered workers together instead of relying only on separate households.

Transportation and technology were therefore the leading causes because they turned other changes into an interconnected market.

prompt: Develop an argument explaining the causes of the decline of Reconstruction governments from 1868 to 1877.
student_response: Reconstruction governments were in the South after the Civil War and they did many things. Some people supported them and other people did not. Elections happened and the governments had officials, but politics was unstable. There were disagreements about how the South should return and who should be allowed to take part. This caused arguments in towns and states. Many white southerners were angry because they thought the new governments were being forced on them. African Americans wanted rights and safety. Northern people had opinions too, though attention changed later. Governments need support in order to continue. If voters become afraid or tired, officials can lose power. Money was also a problem. Rebuilding roads, schools, and buildings cost a lot, and taxes made people complain. Charges of corruption were common. Some charges were fair and some were just political attacks. Violence. Threats during elections. People staying away from the polls. Officials could not always enforce laws outside cities. Over time the situation became different, although it is difficult to know exactly when the decline began. National leaders started thinking about other issues. Economic problems affected the whole country and made Reconstruction seem less important to some voters. Court decisions happened and policies shifted. Eventually southern Democrats controlled their states again and federal soldiers were no longer involved in the same way. The governments declined because support declined. Opposition increased. There were political, social, and economic causes, plus the passage of time. Reconstruction had started with hope but it did not solve every problem, and people disagreed over its goals from the start. In the end the governments were replaced. This ended that part of Reconstruction even though debates about rights continued.

feedback.thesis: Saying the governments declined because support declined and opposition increased names broad categories but does not make a specific, defensible causal claim establishing a line of reasoning.
feedback.contextualization: The opening situates Reconstruction governments after the Civil War amid disputes over southern restoration and political participation.
feedback.evidence: Taxes, corruption charges, violence, economic problems, court decisions, and troop withdrawal are mentioned generally, without at least two specific historical examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response does organize its discussion causally around weakening support, violent opposition, financial complaints, shifting national attention, and reduced enforcement.

thesis: The governments declined because support declined. Opposition increased. There were political, social, and economic causes, plus the passage of time.
contextualization: Reconstruction governments were in the South after the Civil War and they did many things. Some people supported them and other people did not.
evidence: Economic problems affected the whole country and made Reconstruction seem less important to some voters. Court decisions happened and policies shifted.
analysis_reasoning: Violence. Threats during elections. People staying away from the polls. Officials could not always enforce laws outside cities.

feedback.thesis: The response says support declined and opposition increased but does not present a specific, defensible causal claim explaining which causes mattered or how they produced decline.
feedback.contextualization: The opening identifies the post-Civil War South but does not describe broader historical circumstances that frame the decline.
feedback.evidence: Taxes, corruption charges, violence, economic problems, court decisions, and troop withdrawal are mentioned only generally, without at least two specific historical examples.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The essay uses causal categories such as political, social, and economic pressures, but its circular and generalized explanation does not demonstrate complex reasoning.

thesis: The governments declined because support declined. Opposition increased. There were political, social, and economic causes, plus the passage of time.
contextualization: Reconstruction governments were in the South after the Civil War and they did many things.
evidence: Money was also a problem. Rebuilding roads, schools, and buildings cost a lot, and taxes made people complain. Charges of corruption were common.
Economic problems affected the whole country and made Reconstruction seem less important to some voters. Court decisions happened and policies shifted.
analysis_reasoning: Many white southerners were angry because they thought the new governments were being forced on them. African Americans wanted rights and safety. Northern people had opinions too, though attention changed later.

prompt: Develop an argument comparing presidential and congressional approaches to Reconstruction from 1865 to 1877.
student_response: Presidential Reconstruction generally sought quick restoration of the former Confederate states through lenient terms and executive control, whereas congressional Reconstruction imposed stricter conditions and used federal authority to protect freedpeople; both approaches claimed to preserve the Union but disagreed over whether reunion or civil rights should come first. The presidential approach treated the war's end as the main problem. Once southern citizens accepted loyalty and formed governments, national unity could be declared restored. This view allowed many members of the old leadership to return. It expected states to handle voting and local rights with little supervision. Supporters said punishment would prolong sectional hatred and make reunion impossible. The congressional approach distrusted rapid restoration. Legislators argued that letting the same social class regain power would make wartime victory meaningless. They wanted southern governments to meet national conditions before receiving full representation. Congress therefore favored a longer process, stronger federal supervision, and a definition of citizenship that states could not easily remove. It also wanted political participation for formerly enslaved men. The methods reflected different understandings of constitutional power. Presidents claimed that restoring states was an executive responsibility, while legislators claimed authority because each chamber decided whether to seat representatives. At one point the president used his constitutional power to impeach members of Congress who opposed his plan, creating a major separation of powers dispute. This fight made compromise less likely. In practice, neither approach fully secured a stable settlement. Leniency allowed hostile local officials to regain influence, while stricter congressional control depended on continuing northern commitment. Both sides also spoke about republican government and the Constitution. The comparison is therefore not simply kind against cruel. Presidential policy gave highest priority to speed and reconciliation. Congressional policy gave greater priority to restructuring southern politics and enforcing national rights, even at the cost of military supervision and delay.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly contrasts lenient, executive-led restoration with stricter congressional conditions and federal protection of freedpeople while identifying preservation of the Union as a shared goal.
feedback.contextualization: The essay frames both approaches through the end of the war, the restoration of former Confederate states, reunion, and the status of freedpeople.
feedback.evidence: Loyalty-based restoration, the return of old leaders, state control of voting, congressional conditions for representation, national citizenship, political participation, and military supervision support the comparison.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response compares priorities, methods, and constitutional claims, identifies a shared purpose, and qualifies the contrast by explaining the weaknesses of both approaches.

thesis: Presidential Reconstruction generally sought quick restoration of the former Confederate states through lenient terms and executive control, whereas congressional Reconstruction imposed stricter conditions and used federal authority to protect freedpeople; both approaches claimed to preserve the Union but disagreed over whether reunion or civil rights should come first.
contextualization: The presidential approach treated the war's end as the main problem. Once southern citizens accepted loyalty and formed governments, national unity could be declared restored.
evidence: This view allowed many members of the old leadership to return. It expected states to handle voting and local rights with little supervision.

They wanted southern governments to meet national conditions before receiving full representation. Congress therefore favored a longer process, stronger federal supervision, and a definition of citizenship that states could not easily remove.
analysis_reasoning: In practice, neither approach fully secured a stable settlement. Leniency allowed hostile local officials to regain influence, while stricter congressional control depended on continuing northern commitment.

feedback.thesis: The thesis clearly contrasts speedy, lenient executive restoration with stricter congressional conditions and federal protection of freedpeople while identifying reunion as a shared goal.
feedback.contextualization: The essay begins directly with its comparison and does not provide a broader pre-1865 or wartime setting for Reconstruction.
feedback.evidence: Return of former leaders, state control of rights, congressional control over seating representatives, national citizenship, Black male participation, and federal supervision substantiate the comparison despite one reversed impeachment claim.
feedback.analysis_reasoning: The response compares priorities, methods, constitutional claims, and practical limits and avoids reducing the approaches to a simple moral binary.

contextualization: The presidential approach treated the war's end as the main problem.

They wanted southern governments to meet national conditions before receiving full representation. Congress therefore favored a longer process, stronger federal supervision, and a definition of citizenship that states could not easily remove. It also wanted political participation for formerly enslaved men.
Presidents claimed that restoring states was an executive responsibility, while legislators claimed authority because each chamber decided whether to seat representatives.
analysis_reasoning: In practice, neither approach fully secured a stable settlement. Leniency allowed hostile local officials to regain influence, while stricter congressional control depended on continuing northern commitment.
The comparison is therefore not simply kind against cruel.
